summary: I was born in Norwalk, California, in 1926. My family lived there while we were involved in truck farming, which taught me a lot about discipline and responsibility. Growing up in Norwalk, I balanced learning English and maintaining my Japanese cultural traditions. It was the foundation of my early life before the challenges of World War II.

My family originally came from Japan, and our cultural background deeply influenced my upbringing. Although we lived in California, we maintained many Japanese traditions alongside our Buddhist faith. This heritage was a significant part of my identity even as I adapted to life in America.

In addition to our Japanese Buddhist practices, my family attended a Quaker church in Whittier, California. This unique blend of cultural and religious influences shaped my perspective growing up. Whittier was a place where I experienced this intersection of traditions firsthand.

World War II was a very difficult time for my family and me. During the war, my father was interned, and our family was forcibly moved from the Santa Anita Assembly Center to Arkansas. Despite these hardships, I remained focused on continuing my education and later pursued art studies. The war deeply affected our lives but also strengthened my resolve.

During World War II, my family was first taken to the Santa Anita Assembly Center after my father was interned. This place was a temporary holding site before we were relocated to Arkansas. The experience at Santa Anita was part of the difficult journey my family endured during the war.

After being at the Santa Anita Assembly Center, my family was moved to Arkansas during World War II. This relocation was part of the internment process that disrupted our lives. Despite the challenges of living there, I continued my education and held onto my hopes for the future.

After the war, I attended Milwaukee State Teachers College, which was an important step in my education. There, I was exposed to new ideas and artistic influences that helped shape my creative path. This college experience laid the groundwork for my later studies and artistic career.

I continued my artistic education at Black Mountain College, where I encountered many artists and innovative ideas. This experience was pivotal in inspiring me to explore different art forms, including wire sculpture. Black Mountain College was a creative environment that deeply influenced my work.

I served on the San Francisco Arts Commission, which gave me the opportunity to advocate for artists and promote public art. Being part of this organization allowed me to help integrate art into public spaces and government programs. It was a meaningful way to support the arts community in San Francisco.

I also served on the California Arts Council, where I worked to support artists and public art initiatives across the state. This role enabled me to influence how art is incorporated into communities and government projects. It was important to me to help create opportunities for artists and bring art into everyday life.
